text: One cannot quarrel with the majority of Martin’s introductory words, except that two statements are open to correction. The first is that Marlowe was entitled to call himself ‘Sir Christopher Marlowe’ because he had a BA degree. Martin follows Constance Kuriyama in her biography of Marlowe, published in 2002, and this is the basis for his statement. However, as we know, an Englishman was not entitled to call himself ‘Sir’ because he had a BA degree: it was an honorific title, similar to the Latin Domini, and not related to the rank of knighthood or baronet. An example of how this title was used is found in Sir Topas in Twelfth Night. The second statement which resists acceptance is that Marlowe could not have been killed in a political assassination because this reduces most of the scholarly arguments to the level of ‘conspiracy theory’. While one may disagree with scholars like David Riggs, who argues that Marlowe was murdered by spies in the pay of the State, to dismiss all arguments along these lines as theories because they cannot be proved is to close down rather than to open up the discussion.
